SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claim—that jointly training an attention-based encoder with a CTC auxiliary objective mitigates alignment errors and yields competitive performance—is supported by experiments on CSJ and HKUST and comparison to reasonable baselines. There are no noted mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology (attaching CTC to the encoder, joint training/decoding) is technically sound and plausible. However, the summary lacks precise evaluation metrics, statistical significance testing, and details of decoding/LM usage, which are omissions that prevent full technical validation. Overall, these are minor issues that do not undermine the central experimental claims.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses end-to-end ASR, proposing a joint CTC-attention model and demonstrating results on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences that accept speech and sequence modeling advances. It targets the right audience by tackling alignment issues in attention-based ASR and compares to strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC variants, TDNN LF-MMI), using expected evaluation practices and datasets. The problem formulation (joint CTC as auxiliary/regularizer) and positioning relative to related work are appropriate for the venue’s research community.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and its main contributions (joint CTC–attention training/decoding) are stated early. Notation and methodological description appear consistent and well-defined, and related work and hyperparameters are reported. However some presentation details are missing in the provided summary (evaluation metrics are not specified and figures/tables are not described), so there are minor clarity gaps despite overall good writing quality.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates the joint CTC-attention model on two diverse benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several strong baselines, which provides solid empirical support for the main claim that CTC guidance mitigates attention alignment errors. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and limitations (e.g., not outperforming TDNN with LF-MMI and CTC’s dependence on LM/graph decoding) are discussed. However, important reproducibility details are partially missing (evaluation metrics are not specified and code is not mentioned) and no explicit ablation/sensitivity study of the joint objective weighting or decoding components is reported, which prevents a fully comprehensive analysis compared with closely related joint CTC/attention prior work.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets an important problem in end-to-end ASR—irregular attention alignments and associated insertion/deletion errors—by proposing a joint CTC-attention model that leverages CTC as an auxiliary/regularization objective attached to an attention encoder. Demonstrating comparable performance to conventional hybrid systems on CSJ and HKUST without linguistic resources suggests the approach is practically useful and could influence practitioner choices. The idea of combining monotonic CTC constraints with flexible attention is likely to open further research on multi-objective E2E training and be adopted or built upon by others in the ASR community. Its broader implications beyond ASR are limited, however, since the contribution mainly addresses alignment and decoding issues specific to speech recognition.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The central contribution—joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR—is essentially the same hybrid formulation already covered by closely related prior work (e.g., "Joint CTC/attention decoding for end-to-end speech recognition" and other 2017 joint CTC-attention architectures). The paper’s application to Japanese and Mandarin and the observations about ideogram languages and scaling are useful but constitute an incremental application/empirical study rather than a fundamentally new method. The authors report competitive results without linguistic resources, but given the strong overlap with existing joint CTC-attention techniques, the novelty is limited to empirical validation and language-specific observations rather than a genuinely new modeling idea.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against several appropriate baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC variants, TDNN with LF-MMI) on standard corpora (CSJ, HKUST), and acknowledges when a prior method (TDNN LF-MMI) outperforms it. Comparisons appear conducted under comparable data/conditions and hyperparameters are reported, supporting fairness. However, highly related contemporaneous joint CTC-attention works (e.g., other 2017 joint CTC/attention architectures) are noted as relevant in the retrieval context and it is unclear whether direct empirical comparisons to those specific hybrid CTC/attention approaches were made, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR model that leverages both CTC and attention benefits during training and decoding.
Uses CTC as an auxiliary/regularization objective attached to an attention-based encoder to mitigate irregular alignments of attention models.

Demonstrates the proposed joint CTC-attention approach on Japanese and Mandarin Chinese benchmarks and reports comparable performance to conventional hybrid ASR systems without using linguistic resources.
Shows that the joint CTC-attention approach helps avoid insertion and deletion alignment errors present in pure attention-based ASR.
_summary.methodology: Attach a CTC objective to an attention-based encoder and jointly train/decoding with both CTC and attention (a joint CTC-attention network) so that CTC provides monotonic alignment constraints to guide the attention mechanism.
datasets: HKUST Mandarin Chinese conversational telephone speech (MTS)
baselines: CTC with language model (Miao et al., 2016)
TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al., 2016)
limitations: The proposed method did not reach the performance obtained by TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (which leverages phoneme representation and discriminative training).
CTC requires language models and graph-based decoding except with huge training data (stated limitation of CTC).
Attention-based models can produce irregular alignments (repetition and deletion errors) without CTC guidance.
